museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: A museum drawer from the golden-age peak: Q*bert hops into arcades, Atari pushes the 5200 into limited retail, and British homes begin to hear games loading from cassette.
museum.curatorNote: October 1982 is a difficult month to pin down cleanly. Arcade publication dates, trade-show showings, home-console launch windows, and UK microcomputer availability overlap, so this exhibit marks uncertain dates openly rather than pretending every cabinet and cartridge arrived on the same day everywhere.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five moments from a month when the arcade still felt central, but the home computer was beginning to tug games into bedrooms.

items.title: Q*bert enters the record
items.body: Gottlieb's isometric arcade oddity has a copyright publication date of 18 October 1982, though public showing and wider operator availability are often tied to the November AMOA show and later trade reporting.
items.certainty: verified publication date; public availability complicated
items.sourceNote: Q*bert's US copyright registration cites publication on October 18, 1982; Video Games reported direct sales at the AMOA show in November.

items.title: Atari 5200 starts appearing in limited retail
items.body: Atari's premium SuperSystem began appearing in a handful of US department stores around October, bundled with Super Breakout and surrounded by launch-window cartridges. In Britain, this was mostly news from across the Atlantic rather than a local shelf object.
items.sourceNote: Atari 5200 sources describe an October 1982 limited appearance, with broader availability later and some references simplifying this to November 1982.

items.title: Swordquest: EarthWorld begins Atari's contest mythology
items.body: Atari's first Swordquest game tied a cartridge to a comic book and prize contest. It looks, in hindsight, like an industry at its most lavish just before conditions changed.
items.sourceNote: 1982 release summaries list Swordquest: EarthWorld in October; exact retail day not asserted.

items.title: UK microcomputers become household contenders
items.body: By October, the ZX Spectrum, BBC Micro, Dragon 32, VIC-20 and newly marketed Commodore 64 had turned games into something that could arrive on a cassette from a newsagent or mail-order advert.
items.sourceNote: ZX Spectrum launched in April 1982; Dragon 32 in August 1982; Commodore 64 marketing began in August 1982; BBC Micro had launched in late 1981.

items.title: Electronic Games previews 1983 before the crash
items.body: The October 1982 Electronic Games issue is remembered for a 1983 videogame preview mood: confident, busy, and still unaware of how fragile the market would soon look.
items.sourceNote: Original Magazines identifies the October 1982 Electronic Games issue as a 1983 videogame preview.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight October-window releases and launch items. Dates are kept broad where trade records disagree.

items.title: Q*bert
items.body: Gottlieb's diagonal, swearing, pyramid-hopping arcade game. Its October publication date is clear; its wider public/operator rollout is fuzzier.
items.sourceNote: Copyright publication date October 18, 1982; AMOA public sales reported in November.

items.title: Pengo
items.body: Sega's ice-block maze game had a Japanese September release and is listed by some references as reaching North America the following month.
items.sourceNote: VGMdb lists Japan as September 26, 1982; HandWiki-style release summaries place North America in the following month and Europe in December.

items.title: Demon Attack
items.body: Imagic's shooter reached Intellivision in October, part of a wave of third-party cartridge makers treating the home console shelf as a serious battleground.
items.sourceNote: Demon Attack references list the Intellivision release in October 1982.

items.title: Swordquest: EarthWorld
items.body: The first Swordquest cartridge, inseparable from its comic and contest premise.
items.sourceNote: 1982 release summaries list Atari's Swordquest: EarthWorld in October; exact day not asserted.

items.title: Super Breakout
items.body: The Atari 5200 pack-in that made the new console feel both premium and strangely tied to an older arcade lineage.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive lists Super Breakout for the Atari 5200 in the October 1982 launch window.

items.title: Star Raiders
items.body: A prestige Atari name reappearing for the 5200, carrying home-computer space-sim ambition into the console's launch story.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive lists Star Raiders for the Atari 5200 in the October 1982 launch window.

items.title: Missile Command
items.body: A familiar arcade defence game used to make the 5200's new hardware feel immediately legible.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive lists Missile Command for the Atari 5200 in the October 1982 launch window.

items.title: Space Invaders
items.body: An older icon pressed into service for Atari's new premium console, proof that early-1980s launch libraries still leaned heavily on arcade memory.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive lists Space Invaders for the Atari 5200 in the October 1982 launch window.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four objects explain the month: an American premium console, British micros, cassette tapes, and arcade cabinets.

items.title: Atari 5200 SuperSystem
items.body: A powerful but awkward premium console, based closely on Atari's 8-bit computer technology and famous for its analogue controller. In October it was more limited US rollout than UK retail reality.
items.sourceNote: Atari 5200 sources describe October/November 1982 launch timing and limited initial availability.

items.title: ZX Spectrum
items.body: Launched in April, but by October it was becoming a central UK gaming machine: cheap enough to imagine at home, flexible enough to invite bedroom coding.
items.sourceNote: Centre for Computing History lists the ZX Spectrum launch on 23 April 1982.

items.title: Dragon 32 and BBC Micro
items.body: British home computing did not belong to one machine. The BBC Micro carried educational authority; the Welsh Dragon 32 added another serious colour-computer option.
items.sourceNote: Dragon 32 references list August 1982; BBC Micro launched in December 1981.

items.title: Cassette as game delivery
items.body: For UK micro owners, the sound of loading was part of the exhibit: squeal, border stripes, patience, and the real possibility of failure.
items.sourceNote: Included as UK home-computer culture around Spectrum/BBC/Dragon software.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: In 1982 the magazine was not a supplement to the internet. It was the map.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: 1983 Videogame Preview
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image for Electronic Games, October 1982.
items.curatorNote: A confident American videogame magazine looking forward to 1983, still standing inside the arcade-and-console boom.
items.body: A confident American videogame magazine looking forward to 1983, still standing inside the arcade-and-console boom.
items.sourceNote: Original Magazines identifies the October 1982 Electronic Games issue as a 1983 videogame preview.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Arcade and home-computer coverage for UK readers
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image for Computer and Video Games, October 1982.
items.curatorNote: C&VG sat exactly where this exhibit lives: between coin-op fascination and the domestic computer arriving under the television.
items.body: C&VG sat exactly where this exhibit lives: between coin-op fascination and the domestic computer arriving under the television.
items.certainty: issue existence supported by archive context; cover line reconstructed
items.sourceNote: Retro magazine archives document C&VG's early-1980s monthly run; specific cover-line text here is treated as reconstructed.

items.title: Personal Computer World
items.headline: The home micro as serious machine
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image for Personal Computer World, October 1982.
items.curatorNote: Not a games magazine, but an essential UK shelf object for the world that made home computer games possible.
items.body: Not a games magazine, but an essential UK shelf object for the world that made home computer games possible.
items.sourceNote: Centre for Computing History lists Personal Computer World - October 1982.

items.title: Popular Computing Weekly
items.headline: The weekly feed for British micro owners
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image for Popular Computing Weekly, 7 October 1982.
items.curatorNote: A weekly UK pulse: cheaper, faster, and closer to the reader who wanted listings, adverts, and machine gossip now.
items.body: A weekly UK pulse: cheaper, faster, and closer to the reader who wanted listings, adverts, and machine gossip now.
items.sourceNote: A scan of Popular Computing Weekly, Vol. 1 No. 25, dated 7 October 1982, is available online.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: There was no ordinary online life for most players. The network was paper, cassette, club, shop, and school.

items.title: Listings were the download
items.body: A program printed in a magazine could become a game if you typed it correctly and the machine accepted your patience.
items.sourceNote: Included as UK microcomputer culture rather than a single dated claim.

items.title: Mail order was a network
items.body: Adverts, order forms, postal delays, and small software houses acted like a slow, physical internet for early home-computer games.
items.sourceNote: Included as early-1980s software distribution context.

items.title: Arcade knowledge was local
items.body: Strategies travelled by watching over shoulders, reading magazines, and hearing rumours near the cabinet.
items.sourceNote: Arcade culture context.

items.title: Teletext and bulletin boards were edge cases
items.body: Networked information existed, but it was not the normal way a UK child or teenager discovered games in October 1982.
items.sourceNote: Phrased cautiously to avoid overstating online access.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: October 1982 was a split-screen month: arcade brightness on one side, domestic loading noise on the other.

items.title: The arcade still felt like the future
items.body: Cabinets had colour, sound, art, and presence. Q*bert did not look like a home-computer program; it looked like a machine with a personality.

items.title: The home felt newly programmable
items.body: A Spectrum or BBC Micro was not just a game machine. It suggested that games were things a person might type, save, trade, and understand.

items.title: The UK experience was cassette-shaped
items.body: Loading was part of play: the squeal, the wait, the fear that a volume knob could ruin everything.

items.title: The boom felt normal from inside it
items.body: From October 1982, the coming crash was not yet the story in the room. The story was more games, more machines, more magazines, more choice.
